content.text: Security Patch Cadence for Claude Dependencies

Claude-based applications depend on a stack of libraries and infrastructure components — the Anthropic SDK, HTTP clients, authentication libraries, container base images, and orchestration frameworks. Each of these carries its own vulnerability surface, and new CVEs are disclosed continuously. A security patch cadence defines how quickly a team must assess, test, and deploy patches for discovered vulnerabilities depending on their severity. Without a defined cadence, security patches are treated like feature work: deferred, deprioritised, and batched into whenever the next release happens to ship.

attrs.alt: Security patch cadence by severity: Critical CVSSv3 9-10 must be assessed and hotfixed within 24 hours, High within 72 hours, Medium in the next scheduled 30-day release, Low batched into 90-day quarterly maintenance

content.text: Severity Classification for AI Application Dependencies

CVSSv3 scores provide a baseline severity signal, but they must be adjusted for the specific deployment context of an AI application. A vulnerability rated CRITICAL by CVSS might have near-zero exploitability in a deployment where the affected code path is never reached. Conversely, a MEDIUM-rated vulnerability in a component that sits on the hot path for every API request warrants expedited treatment. The classification step is not mechanical CVSS lookup — it requires contextual assessment.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class CVSSSeverity(str, Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"   # 9.0-10.0
    HIGH     = "high"       # 7.0-8.9
    MEDIUM   = "medium"     # 4.0-6.9
    LOW      = "low"        # 0.1-3.9

class ExposureContext(str, Enum):
    INTERNET_FACING   = "internet_facing"    # component reachable from internet
    INTERNAL_ONLY     = "internal_only"      # component reachable only within VPC
    DEAD_CODE         = "dead_code"          # code path never executed in this deployment
    AUTH_GATED        = "auth_gated"         # requires valid auth before reaching component

@dataclass
class VulnerabilityAssessment:
    cve_id: str
    component: str
    cvss_score: float
    base_severity: CVSSSeverity
    exposure: ExposureContext
    has_known_exploit: bool
    affects_api_hot_path: bool   # True if component is on every API request path

    def effective_severity(self) -> CVSSSeverity:
        """Adjust base severity based on deployment context."""
        if self.exposure == ExposureContext.DEAD_CODE:
            # Dead code: downgrade by one level — vulnerability unreachable
            order = [CVSSSeverity.LOW, CVSSSeverity.MEDIUM, CVSSSeverity.HIGH, CVSSSeverity.CRITICAL]
            idx = order.index(self.base_severity)
            return order[max(0, idx - 1)]
        if self.has_known_exploit and self.exposure == ExposureContext.INTERNET_FACING:
            # Known exploit on internet-facing component: upgrade to CRITICAL regardless of score
            return CVSSSeverity.CRITICAL
        if self.exposure == ExposureContext.AUTH_GATED and self.base_severity == CVSSSeverity.CRITICAL:
            # Auth-gated path reduces exploitability: downgrade to HIGH
            return CVSSSeverity.HIGH
        return self.base_severity

    def patch_deadline_hours(self) -> int | None:
        """Returns hours, or None for quarterly batching."""
        match self.effective_severity():
            case CVSSSeverity.CRITICAL: return 24
            case CVSSSeverity.HIGH:     return 72
            case CVSSSeverity.MEDIUM:   return 30 * 24   # 30 days
            case CVSSSeverity.LOW:      return None      # batch into quarterly

The downgrade for dead code is safe only if the dead code determination is verified — not assumed. A component that appears unused in a staging environment may be instantiated conditionally based on a feature flag or request parameter that was not active during testing. Dead code assessments must be backed by static analysis or code coverage data from production traffic, not developer intuition.
The 24-Hour Critical Patch Process

A 24-hour patch window for CRITICAL vulnerabilities is aggressive. It requires a pre-agreed process that can be activated without normal release ceremony. The steps — assessment, patch acquisition, testing, staging deploy, production deploy — must each be scoped to fit within the 24-hour window while maintaining the minimum necessary quality gate. Normal release processes take days; the critical path takes hours.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class CriticalPatchOperation:
    cve_id: str
    component: str
    patched_version: str
    started_at: datetime

    # Checkpoints (each must be timestamped as completed)
    assessment_completed_at: datetime | None = None
    patch_acquired_at: datetime | None = None
    unit_tests_passed_at: datetime | None = None
    staging_deployed_at: datetime | None = None
    staging_smoke_test_at: datetime | None = None
    production_deployed_at: datetime | None = None

    def elapsed_hours(self, as_of: datetime | None = None) -> float:
        ref = as_of or datetime.utcnow()
        return (ref - self.started_at).total_seconds() / 3600

    def sla_remaining_hours(self) -> float:
        return max(0.0, 24.0 - self.elapsed_hours())

    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        return self.production_deployed_at is not None

    def checkpoint_summary(self) -> list[str]:
        steps = [
            ("Assessment",      self.assessment_completed_at),
            ("Patch acquired",  self.patch_acquired_at),
            ("Unit tests",      self.unit_tests_passed_at),
            ("Staging deploy",  self.staging_deployed_at),
            ("Staging smoke",   self.staging_smoke_test_at),
            ("Prod deploy",     self.production_deployed_at),
        ]
        return [
            f"{'[x]' if ts else '[ ]'} {name}"
            + (f" ({ts.strftime('%H:%M UTC')})" if ts else "")
            for name, ts in steps
        ]

# Abbreviated testing protocol for critical patches
CRITICAL_TEST_GATES = [
    "Unit tests for changed component only (not full suite)",
    "API smoke tests: 10 requests across all major endpoint types",
    "Authentication path test: valid + invalid token scenarios",
    "Claude API call succeeds end-to-end (single model call)",
    "No new error log lines in staging within 5 minutes of deploy",
]

The abbreviated test gate list for critical patches is a deliberate trade-off: skipping the full test suite saves 45–90 minutes, which is significant against a 24-hour window. The gate list covers the failure modes most likely to be introduced by a dependency version bump: broken auth, broken API surface, and log noise indicating unexpected behaviour. Full regression testing runs immediately after production deployment, and rollback is triggered automatically if error rates exceed a 2x baseline threshold within the first hour.
Dependency Inventory and Scanning Automation

A patch cadence is only actionable if the team knows which versions of which dependencies are deployed in production at any given moment. Maintaining this inventory manually is error-prone and quickly goes stale as deployments occur. The inventory must be generated from the deployed artifacts — container image SBOMs (software bill of materials), lock files in the repository, and the output of dependency audit tools — rather than from documentation.

Production scans run every 6 hours because CVE databases are updated continuously and a vulnerability disclosed at midnight should be known to the team before the next business day. The 6-hour window is also aligned with the 24-hour critical patch window — a critical vulnerability disclosed at 11 PM will be detected by the 5 AM scan, leaving 19 hours to patch before the SLA expires.
SDK and Model Client Version Policy

The Anthropic SDK is a first-party dependency with its own release cadence and security advisories. Beyond generic dependency scanning, Claude applications should maintain a specific policy for how far behind the current SDK version they are permitted to run. An application running three major versions behind the SDK may be missing security fixes, deprecated API parameters with known vulnerabilities, or TLS certificate validation changes.
Version Gap
Policy
Exception Process
Current or one minor behind
Allowed; upgrade at next scheduled release
None needed
Two minor versions behind
Warning; upgrade required within 30 days
Engineering manager sign-off
One major version behind
Non-compliant; upgrade required within 14 days
VP Engineering sign-off + documented risk acceptance
Two+ major versions behind
Blocked from production deployment
No exception available; upgrade required

The "blocked from production deployment" policy for two-plus major versions is enforced at the CI level, not by policy alone. A CI check that reads the SDK version from the lock file and fails the build if the gap is too large makes the policy self-enforcing without requiring manual compliance checks. Policies that rely on engineers voluntarily flagging non-compliance tend to erode over time; policies enforced by automated gates hold.
Tracking and Reporting Patch Compliance

The patch cadence policy only has force if compliance is tracked and reported. A monthly security posture report covering open vulnerabilities by severity, time-in-backlog against SLA, and any exceptions granted provides visibility to the security team and engineering leadership. This report also serves as the source of evidence in a security audit — an auditor asking about patch management practices will want to see not just a policy document, but a record showing the policy is being followed.
Rollback Planning for Failed Security Patches

Security patches sometimes introduce regressions. A dependency version bump that closes a CVE may also change an API signature, alter a default behaviour, or break compatibility with another library. The urgency of the security patch can create pressure to deploy without adequate testing, and the first sign of a regression is often increased error rates in production. Rollback planning must be completed before the patch deployment begins — not decided in the middle of an incident.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum

class RollbackTrigger(str, Enum):
    ERROR_RATE_SPIKE  = "error_rate_spike"      # >2x baseline within 15 min
    LATENCY_SPIKE     = "latency_spike"          # P99 >3x baseline
    HEALTH_CHECK_FAIL = "health_check_fail"      # /health endpoint returning 5xx
    MANUAL_TRIGGER    = "manual_trigger"         # on-call engineer judgment

@dataclass
class PatchRollbackPlan:
    cve_id: str
    component: str
    patched_version: str
    previous_version: str       # known-good version to roll back to
    rollback_command: str        # exact command, tested in staging
    error_rate_baseline: float   # p99 error rate before patch deploy
    latency_baseline_ms: float   # p99 latency before patch deploy
    monitoring_window_minutes: int = 60   # how long to monitor post-deploy

    def should_rollback(
        self,
        current_error_rate: float,
        current_p99_latency_ms: float,
        trigger: RollbackTrigger | None = None,
    ) -> bool:
        if trigger == RollbackTrigger.MANUAL_TRIGGER:
            return True
        if trigger == RollbackTrigger.HEALTH_CHECK_FAIL:
            return True
        if current_error_rate > 2 * self.error_rate_baseline:
            return True
        if current_p99_latency_ms > 3 * self.latency_baseline_ms:
            return True
        return False

    def rollback_record(self, initiated_by: str) -> dict:
        return {
            "cve_id": self.cve_id,
            "rolled_back_from": self.patched_version,
            "rolled_back_to": self.previous_version,
            "initiated_by": initiated_by,
            "initiated_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
            "note": "Security patch rolled back due to regression. CVE remains open — re-patch required.",
        }

The rollback record's note field is critical: it must explicitly state that the CVE remains open after rollback. A rolled-back security patch does not resolve the vulnerability — it restores the system to a known-stable state while the team investigates the regression. The CVE must re-enter the patch queue immediately, with a new deadline calculated from the rollback time. A rollback that takes two days to investigate and re-patch still needs to meet the original severity SLA from the time the rollback was initiated.
Handling Zero-Days and Advisory Embargoes

Coordinated vulnerability disclosure means that some vulnerabilities are shared with vendors before public disclosure, under embargo. A vendor may contact the team directly about a vulnerability in a shared dependency, with a patch available and a disclosure date set 7-14 days out. During the embargo period, the vulnerability exists, the patch exists, and the team is not permitted to publicly discuss it — but they are expected to deploy the patch before public disclosure.
Embargo receipt:

When an embargoed advisory is received, it is handled under strict need-to-know. Only the engineers who need to deploy the patch see the advisory details. The advisory is not shared in general engineering channels before public disclosure.
Pre-disclosure deployment:

The goal is to have the patch deployed before public disclosure, so that the vulnerability is already closed when the CVE is published. This requires testing and staging deployment during the embargo period, followed by production deployment 24-48 hours before the disclosure date.
Post-disclosure documentation:

After public disclosure, the CVE can be documented in the normal patch compliance record with its actual detection-to-patch timeline. The embargo period does not reset the clock — the detection timestamp is when the advisory was received, not when the CVE was public.
Zero-day without a patch:

If a zero-day is disclosed publicly with no patch yet available, the response is mitigation rather than patching: disabling the affected feature, increasing monitoring on the affected code path, or applying a WAF rule to block known exploit patterns. Document the mitigation as a temporary risk acceptance with a review date.
Patch Testing Strategy for AI Inference Components

Patching a general-purpose HTTP library and patching a library that sits on the model inference call path carry different risk profiles. A regression in a general HTTP library might cause some API requests to fail. A regression in the inference path can produce silently incorrect outputs — responses that look syntactically valid but contain different content than before the patch. Standard test suites check for errors; they do not check for output quality changes.
Component Type
Test Gate
Additional Check for Inference Path
HTTP client / networking
API smoke tests + error rate baseline
n/a
Authentication / token handling
Auth path tests + token expiry scenarios
n/a
Anthropic SDK (model client)
Full unit tests + integration tests
Golden-set output comparison: 20 canned prompts, verify response structure unchanged
Serialisation / parsing (JSON, protobuf)
Round-trip tests on sample payloads
Verify message_start and message_delta streaming events parse correctly
Container base image
Full integration test suite in staging
End-to-end inference call with multi-turn conversation sample

The golden-set output comparison for SDK patches checks that a fixed set of 20 canned prompts produce responses with the same structural properties as before the patch (same stop reason, same content structure, correct token count range) — not identical text. Model outputs are non-deterministic; checking for identical text would produce false failures on every run. The structural comparison catches regressions like a streaming parser change that truncates the last token, or an SDK update that accidentally changes the default stop sequence.
Summary

Security patch cadence for Claude application dependencies requires four elements: severity classification adjusted for deployment context (not raw CVSS scores alone), time-bound SLA windows (24h critical, 72h high, 30 days medium, 90 days low), automated dependency scanning every 6 hours in production, and an SDK version policy enforced at CI time. Monthly posture reports track compliance and serve as audit evidence. The abbreviated test gate for critical patches accepts a deliberate risk trade-off — partial testing to meet the 24-hour window, with full regression running immediately after production deployment.
